questions.q: A smooth atlas on $M$ is a collection of charts $\{(U_\alpha,\varphi_\alpha)\}$ covering $M$ such that:
choices: each $\varphi_\alpha$ is a linear isomorphism
the transition maps $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ are $C^\infty$ diffeomorphisms between open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$
the charts are pairwise disjoint
$M$ is compact
questions.explain: Smoothness of transition maps between Euclidean patches is exactly what lifts calculus from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $M$. No disjointness, compactness, or linearity is required.

questions.q: On $S^2$ with the two-chart stereographic atlas from $N$ and $S$, the overlap $U_N\cap U_S$ is $S^2$ minus how many points?
questions.explain: The north chart misses $N$ and the south chart misses $S$, so the overlap is $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$ — two missing points.

questions.q: The transition map between the two stereographic charts on $S^2$ is:
choices: the identity map
inversion in the unit circle, $(u,v)\mapsto (u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$
a rotation by $\pi$
only continuous, not smooth
questions.explain: Composing the two stereographic projections gives $(u,v)\mapsto(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$, a $C^\infty$ diffeomorphism of $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$.

hard.q: The circle $S^1$ admits a smooth atlas with two charts. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing that a single chart cannot cover $S^1$?
choices: A single chart would require a homeomorphism $S^1\to\mathbb{R}$, but $S^1$ is compact and $\mathbb{R}$ is not
A single chart is disallowed by the definition of manifold regardless of topology
No open subset of $\mathbb{R}$ can be diffeomorphic to an arc of $S^1$
The transition map of a single-chart atlas would not be smooth
hard.explain: A single chart would require a homeomorphism from all of $S^1$ onto an open subset of $\mathbb{R}$. But $S^1$ is compact and a continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is a homeomorphism onto its image; no open subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact, so no such homeomorphism exists.

hard.q: The torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$ carries a smooth atlas derived from the product of two two-chart atlases on $S^1$. What is the minimum number of charts in such a product atlas?
hard.explain: Each factor $S^1$ needs at least $2$ charts, so the product atlas has $2\times 2 = 4$ charts of the form $U_i\times V_j$.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in verifying that two charts $(U,\varphi)$ and $(V,\psi)$ are smoothly compatible?
choices: Check $U\cap V\ne\emptyset$, then verify $\psi\circ\varphi^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$ on $\varphi(U\cap V)$, then verify $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$ on $\psi(U\cap V)$
Check $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$, then verify $U$ and $V$ are disjoint
Verify $\varphi=\psi$ on $U\cap V$, then check surjectivity
Verify $U\cap V=\emptyset$, then the charts are automatically compatible
hard.explain: Two charts are smoothly compatible when their overlap is open (possibly empty) and both transition maps $\psi\circ\varphi^{-1}$ and $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ are $C^\infty$. If the overlap is empty they are trivially compatible, skipping the smoothness check.

questions.q: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ and $p\in M$, the tangent space $T_pM$ is:
choices: an $n$-dimensional real vector space
the tangent line to a fixed curve through $p$
a subset of $M$ near $p$
the kernel of some linear map on $M$
questions.explain: In any chart the coordinate vectors $\partial/\partial x^1,\dots,\partial/\partial x^n$ form a basis, so $T_pM$ is an $n$-dimensional real vector space.

questions.q: What is the dimension of the tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$?

Under a chart change $y=y(x)$, a tangent vector with components $X^i$ becomes $\tilde X^j = (\partial y^j/\partial x^i)X^i$. If $X=(2,0)$ in coordinates $x$ and the Jacobian at the point is $\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$, what is $\tilde X^1$?

hard.q: A smooth curve $\gamma:(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\to M$ defines a tangent vector $\gamma'(0)\in T_pM$ as a derivation on smooth functions. Which statement correctly orders the steps in showing this derivation satisfies the Leibniz rule?
choices: Compute $\gamma'(0)(fg)$ via the chain rule; apply the product rule for real functions; substitute $f(p)$ and $g(p)$
Verify $\gamma'(0)$ is linear first, then separately check Leibniz without using the chain rule
Assume Leibniz holds by definition of manifold, then verify linearity
Use the chart map to reduce to $\mathbb{R}^n$, then apply Leibniz for partial derivatives before pulling back
hard.explain: $\gamma'(0)(fg)=\frac{d}{dt}\big|_{t=0}(fg\circ\gamma)=f(p)\,g'(0)+g(p)\,f'(0)=f(p)\,\gamma'(0)(g)+g(p)\,\gamma'(0)(f)$ by the real product rule, confirming Leibniz directly without passing to a chart.

hard.q: Let $f:M\to N$ be a smooth map between manifolds of dimensions $3$ and $5$. What is the dimension of $\ker(df_p)$ if $df_p$ has rank $2$?
hard.explain: By the rank-nullity theorem applied to $df_p:T_pM\to T_{f(p)}N$, $\dim\ker(df_p)=\dim T_pM-\operatorname{rank}(df_p)=3-2=1$.

hard.q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that the tangent bundle $TS^2$ is not trivial (i.e., $TS^2\ncong S^2\times\mathbb{R}^2$)?
choices: The hairy ball theorem: every smooth vector field on $S^2$ must vanish somewhere, so no global frame exists
$S^2$ is compact, and compact manifolds never have trivial tangent bundles
A rank-$2$ bundle over a $2$-manifold is always non-trivial
$TS^2$ has the wrong Euler characteristic to be trivial
hard.explain: A trivial rank-$n$ bundle admits $n$ everywhere-linearly-independent global sections, i.e., a global frame. The hairy ball theorem asserts every tangent vector field on $S^2$ vanishes somewhere, so no non-vanishing global section exists, let alone a full frame. Hence $TS^2$ is non-trivial.

questions.q: A smooth map $f\colon M^m\to N^n$ is an immersion at $p$ iff $df_p$:
choices: is surjective
is injective (equivalently, has rank $m$)
has rank $n$
is the zero map
questions.explain: Immersion = injective differential, rank $m$. Submersion is the surjective case with rank $n$.

questions.q: By the regular value theorem, if $c$ is a regular value of $g\colon\mathbb{R}^{n+1}\to\mathbb{R}$ then $g^{-1}(c)$ is a smooth manifold of dimension $n$. For $g(x)=\|x\|^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^4$ and $c=1$, what is the dimension of $g^{-1}(1)=S^3$?
questions.explain: $g\colon\mathbb{R}^4\to\mathbb{R}$, and $1$ is regular, so $g^{-1}(1)$ has dimension $4-1=3$.

questions.q: Whitney's embedding theorem states that every smooth $n$-manifold admits a smooth embedding into:
choices: $\mathbb{R}^n$
$\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$
$\mathbb{R}^{2n}$
no Euclidean space in general
questions.explain: Whitney (1944): every smooth $n$-manifold embeds smoothly in $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$, and immerses in $\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}$.

hard.q: The figure-eight $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(t)=(\sin 2t,\sin t)$, is an injective immersion but not an embedding. Which property fails?
choices: $f$ is not injective: $f(0)=f(\pi)$
$f$ is not an immersion: $df_t$ is zero at some $t$
$f$ is not a topological embedding: the induced topology on $f(\mathbb{R})$ differs from the subspace topology of $\mathbb{R}^2$ near the crossing point
$f(\mathbb{R})$ is not a manifold of any dimension
hard.explain: $f$ is injective and $df_t$ is everywhere non-zero, so it is an injective immersion. However, the image is a figure-eight whose crossing point has a neighbourhood in $f(\mathbb{R})$ that is not homeomorphic to an interval: nearby points in the domain map to four branches meeting at the origin, whereas the subspace topology sees all four. Thus $f$ is not a homeomorphism onto its image and fails to be an embedding.

hard.q: Let $g:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $g(x,y,z)=(x^2+y^2+z^2,\, x+y+z)$. At the point $p=(1,0,0)$, what is the rank of $dg_p$?
hard.explain: $dg_p = \begin{pmatrix}2&0&0\\1&1&1\end{pmatrix}$. The first row has leading entry $2$ and the second has entry $1$ in the second column; the two rows are linearly independent, so $\operatorname{rank}(dg_p)=2$, making $p$ a regular point.

hard.q: By the constant-rank theorem, if $f:M^m\to N^n$ has constant rank $r$ near $p$, what is the local normal form of $f$?
choices: $(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto(x^1,\dots,x^m,0,\dots,0)$ in suitable charts
$(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto(x^1,\dots,x^r,0,\dots,0)$ in suitable charts
$(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto(x^1,\dots,x^n)$ whenever $m\ge n$
$(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto((x^1)^2,\dots,(x^r)^2,0,\dots,0)$
hard.explain: The constant-rank theorem (local form): choosing adapted charts around $p$ and $f(p)$, $f$ looks like the standard projection–injection $(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto(x^1,\dots,x^r,0,\dots,0)\in\mathbb{R}^n$. The first $r$ coordinates are kept; the rest are sent to zero.
